core_meaning: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> covers a wide range: 'it's okay / I'm fine / no problem / don't worry.' It functions both as a reassurance ('everything is alright') and as a yes/no check ('are you okay?'). Crucially, it can also mean 'no thanks, I'm good' — a polite refusal — which trips up many learners.

sentences.jp: <ruby>転<rt>こ</rt></ruby>んじゃったけど、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>だよ、ちょっと<ruby>擦<rt>す</rt></ruby>り<ruby>傷<rt>きず</rt></ruby>ついただけ。
sentences.translation: I fell, but I'm fine — just a little scrape.
sentences.notes: Classic use as self-reassurance after something minor happens; the <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>だよ pattern is very natural in casual speech.

sentences.jp: <ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>の<ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>ですか？
sentences.translation: Are you okay for tomorrow's meeting?
sentences.notes: Used as a check-in question — 'are you free / is it okay?' — very common in workplace or scheduling contexts.

sentences.jp: 「もう<ruby>一杯<rt>いっぱい</rt></ruby>いかがですか？」「あ、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です。」
sentences.translation: 'Would you like another cup?' 'Oh, I'm good, thanks.'
sentences.notes: This is the refusal use: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です here means 'no thank you' — one of the trickiest nuances for learners.

sentences.jp: <ruby>初<rt>はじ</rt></ruby>めてにしては<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>だったんじゃない？
sentences.translation: Wasn't that pretty decent for a first try?
sentences.notes: Shows a slightly different shade: 'not bad / passable' — <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> can mean something met an acceptable standard.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>書類<rt>しょるい</rt></ruby>は<ruby>本日中<rt>ほんじつちゅう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>提出<rt>ていしゅつ</rt></ruby>しなくても<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>でしょうか。
sentences.translation: Would it be alright if I didn't submit these documents within today?
sentences.notes: In formal written/spoken contexts, <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>でしょうか is a polite way to ask for permission or check if something is acceptable.

dont_use: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> sounds casual-to-neutral, so in very stiff formal writing (contracts, official documents) it can feel out of place — prefer <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ございません or <ruby>差<rt>さ</rt></ruby>し<ruby>支<rt>つか</rt></ruby>えありません. Also, don't assume <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> always means 'yes' when someone asks if you need help — it often means 'no, I'm fine without it.' Finally, avoid overusing it as filler agreement (for 'I understand' or 'got it,' <ruby>わかりました<rt>わかりました</rt></ruby> is cleaner).

confused_with.contrast: A learner might say <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない when checking on a friend who got hurt, but this sounds analytical and cold — it assesses a situation logically ('there is no problem') rather than expressing human concern or comfort. <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> carries warmth and is the natural choice for personal reassurance, while <ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない fits better for technical or task-based contexts, e.g. '<ruby>スケジュール<rt>すけじゅーる</rt></ruby>は<ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない' (the schedule is fine).

confusion_set.use_when: Reassuring someone, checking on wellbeing, politely declining an offer, or saying something is acceptable.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>転<rt>こ</rt></ruby>んだけど<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>！

confusion_set.use_when: Stating there is objectively no issue with a plan, schedule, or task — more logical, less personal.
confusion_set.example: この<ruby>設計<rt>せっけい</rt></ruby>は<ruby>技術的<rt>ぎじゅつてき</rt></ruby>に<ruby>問題<rt>もんだい</rt></ruby>ない。

confusion_set.use_when: Saying you personally don't mind something or are unbothered — more about inner composure than external safety.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>辛<rt>から</rt></ruby>いもの、<ruby>平気<rt>へいき</rt></ruby>だよ。

frequency: <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> is extremely common in everyday spoken Japanese — one of the most frequently heard words in casual and standard conversation. It appears across all age groups and is safe to use in most situations short of very formal written Japanese.
anki_hint: Remember the refusal trap: when a waiter asks if you want more and you say <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です, you're saying 'no thanks' — <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> can mean 'I'm good (and don't need it),' not just 'yes, everything is fine.'
